questions.stem: What is the average time complexity of dictionary lookup (d[key]) in Python?
options: O(n) — it searches through all keys one by one
O(1) average — the hash of the key directly maps to a slot, requiring no comparison with other keys
O(log n) — Python uses a binary search tree internally
O(n²) — due to hash collision resolution
questions.explanation: CPython dict uses a hash table: hash(key) computes an integer, which maps to a slot index. If no collision, lookup finds the value immediately — O(1). Collisions (same hash slot) require probing additional slots — O(1) amortised in practice since collision rate is kept low (load factor < 2/3). This is why tokenizer vocabulary lookups (50,000 entries) and model state dict lookups are instant.

questions.stem: What does dict.get(key, default) do differently from dict[key]?
options: get() is an older syntax; dict[key] is the modern equivalent
dict[key] raises KeyError if the key is absent; dict.get(key, default) returns default (or None) without raising an exception — safer when key presence is uncertain
get() is faster than dict[key] for large dictionaries
get() creates the key if it doesn't exist; dict[key] does not
questions.explanation: d['missing_key'] raises KeyError — your program crashes. d.get('missing_key', 0) returns 0 silently. In ML: counts.get(token, 0) + 1 safely counts token frequencies. defaultdict(int) is even cleaner for counting: counts[token] += 1 initialises to 0 automatically. state_dict.get('module.layer.weight', None) handles the DataParallel 'module.' prefix issue gracefully.

questions.stem: What is a Python dictionary comprehension, and give an ML use case?
options: A concise way to create dictionaries: {key_expr: value_expr for item in iterable} — e.g. creating an inverse vocabulary map {token_id: word for word, token_id in vocab.items()}
A Python built-in for reading dictionary files (like spell-check wordlists)
A way to merge two dictionaries into one
A method that generates a human-readable description of a dictionary's contents
questions.explanation: {k: v for k, v in items} creates a dict in one expression. ML use cases: inverse vocabulary for decoding {id: token for token, id in vocab.items()}; filter state dict {k: v for k, v in state.items() if 'weight' in k}; metric dict {f'val_{m}': score for m, score in metrics.items()}. Comprehensions are cleaner than building dicts with loops.

questions.stem: What is Python's defaultdict, and when does it improve code clarity?
options: A dict with a default value set for ALL keys at creation time
A dict that automatically creates a default value for missing keys using a factory function — eliminates the need for 'if key not in d: d[key] = default_value' checks
A read-only dict where values cannot be changed after insertion
A dict that is ordered by insertion order (standard dicts are unordered)
questions.explanation: defaultdict(list) creates a dict where d[missing_key] returns [] and inserts it. Without: if token not in index: index[token] = []; index[token].append(doc_id). With defaultdict(list): index[token].append(doc_id). Common factories: defaultdict(int) for counting, defaultdict(list) for grouping, defaultdict(set) for unique grouping. In ML: grouping predictions by class: preds_by_class[label].append(score).

questions.stem: When loading a PyTorch model checkpoint saved from a DataParallel model, why might load_state_dict() fail, and how do you fix it?
options: The checkpoint is compressed and needs to be decompressed first

DataParallel adds a 'module.' prefix to all keys (e.g. 'module.layer.weight'). If loading into a non-DataParallel model, keys don't match — fix by stripping the prefix: {k.replace('module.', ''): v for k, v in state.items()}
DataParallel saves weights in float64; you must convert to float32 first
DataParallel checkpoints can only be loaded on the same number of GPUs they were saved on
questions.explanation: torch.nn.DataParallel wraps the model and adds 'module.' to every parameter name. state_dict() yields keys like 'module.transformer.h.0.attn.weight'. Loading this into a bare model expects 'transformer.h.0.attn.weight' — KeyError. Fix: new_state = {k[7:] if k.startswith('module.') else k: v for k, v in old_state.items()}. Or use strict=False to ignore mismatches (less safe).

questions.stem: What does Python's LRU cache (@functools.lru_cache) do and what is a practical ML use case?
options: Caches function results keyed by arguments — subsequent calls with the same arguments return the cached result instantly without recomputing
Automatically distributes function calls across multiple CPU cores
Converts a function to run asynchronously in the background
Limits the function to run at most N times per second (rate limiting)
questions.explanation: @lru_cache(maxsize=128) caches the return value for each unique set of arguments. Cache evicts least-recently-used entries when full. In ML: @lru_cache(maxsize=None) on tokenize(text) so repeated calls with the same text (common in evaluation) return instantly. @lru_cache on load_config(path) ensures the config file is only read once even if called from multiple places. Arguments must be hashable (not lists/dicts — use tuples instead).
